speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about the young American or the American that was your contact. You said that you didn't know anybody else at that point. When did you go up to Nuremberg to testify?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been either November or December 1947, because it was shortly before I came to the States. And I left Germany for the United States in January. So it had to be either probably December of 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay for the whole trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Just for the couple of days of testimony.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were living at the time at Frankfurt. How did you get to Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose with two-- I'm trying to think whether we went by train or maybe they picked us up. I think they picked us-- must have picked us up in Frankfurt by car and took us to Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And upon arrival, how did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderfully. It was very-- everybody was very nice and very kind. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember somebody saying, there's a Czech general here. And since you've got girls that are Czechoslovakian, we'll have to introduce you. And I was so embarrassed because I realized I had a complete mental block. I could not speak Czech. I couldn't-- couldn't believe that I forgot the language that I had spoken for so many years. It was just a complete block. And I-- I remember being very embarrassed about it. And so we testified at the trial for about two days, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then we went back to Frankfurt for a short while and from there to Bremerhaven to-- to come to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just for clarification purposes, you said that they knew you were Czech. Yet you were coming to America under a German--

speaker: interviewee
text: But nobody-- nobody questioned that. Nobody question-- really, nobody questioned it. That-- they didn't-- first of all, at the Nuremberg trials, nobody knew that we are going to the United States with-- as German citizens. Nobody knew that. So as far as they were concerned, we were Czech, and we always were. It's just for the-- for that trip. We were Czech.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, oh-- testifying with you was whom else?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister Elizabeth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you describe your day in court?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember being in court and Alfried Krupp and some of-- some other men sitting there with earphones on, and they were very dour looking. And they just asked us to describe what kind of work we did and what the living conditions were, what we did, whether we got paid for it or not. And-- and-- and then questions asked. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was-- that was about it. You know, it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were testifying against?

speaker: interviewee
text: Krupp-- Alfried Krupp. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, he was the owner? What was his position for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the relation for the Krupp?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is the owner of the Krupp factories-- ammunition factories. I guess it's the largest ammunition factory in Germany-- prominent men.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were called as a witness to testify for what purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we worked as slave labor for Krupp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever seen Alfried Krupp before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only at-- at the trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you know that that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when we came in, they told us who the accused are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anybody else on-- at the same trial with Mr. Krupp?

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- yes, there were some other people, but I don't recall who they were. I really can't tell you who they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your day in court, you said it lasted two days. Do you know anybody else in the courtroom? Was anybody else recognizable? What about the judge, or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The judge, yes. There were the judge, named Telford Taylor-- very pleasant man. And I remember when I got to Pittsburgh, when I came to the State and got to [INAUDIBLE], I had a very nice letter from him thanking me for testifying and offering any help should I-- should we need it in the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of the attorneys? Were you talking to the attorneys--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --on either side?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I don't remember talking to anybody, you know, privately outside the court.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your impression of the trial attorneys, the way they questioned you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought they were very efficient. I didn't have much courtroom experience, so I really cannot compare to. But it seemed like they were very efficient.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did they question you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were fluent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. You know what? They-- we spoke in English. I just realized. We spoke in English. We did not want to speak German. Although we spoke German fluently, we did not want to speak German. So we-- we spoke German probably more fluently at that time than English, but we decided that we are going to speak English.

speaker: interviewer
text: The time that you weren't in court during those two days, what were-- did you do? Did they put you up while--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think they put us up in a hotel, I suppose. I don't really remember too much about it. It's a big blur.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism in Nuremberg when you were up there testify?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because we didn't have much contact except for the American lawyer who picked us up and put us up and-- and-- and then the people in the courtroom. And so we didn't-- didn't have much contact with other-- with Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your most memorable moment at the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's hard to say. I guess just the first-- first time we stepped into the courtroom. Not having been in a courtroom before, it was very impressive and very-- at the same time very intimidating. But I suppose that-- that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take for you to testify?

speaker: interviewee
text: About two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn about the outcome of the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: We learned while we were in the States, through newspaper or radio broadcast, or what-- I think it must have been newspaper-- much later-- that-- that he has been sentenced to a certain amount of years in prison. But-- but it was much later. It was in the States already when we learned about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about the outcome?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess I was-- was satisfying to know that at least he had-- he has been sentenced to some years in prison-- that he just didn't get away scot-free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep in touch with anyone from the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not. I wish I had. The judge-- Judge Telford Taylor in his letter said we should keep in touch, but I didn't. I guess I just wanted to forget everything that was connected to that life. I didn't-- and-- so I didn't keep in touch.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived in America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date and year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in United States on February 5, 1948. We left Bremerhaven in January. I can't tell you what date. Must have been January-- took us three weeks to get there, so it must have been middle of January. We came on a ship that was a troop carrier that was converted, and we hit a very rough sea-- in-- the North Sea. We had a terrible storm. The engine broke down in the-- in the-- on the ship, and the engineer could not fix it. Everybody was trying. And then, my future husband fixed the ship-- fixed the engine. He was radio officer on the ship. And a lot of the people had to send telegrams to their relatives telling them that the ship will be late, because they had these relatives waiting for them at-- in New York at arrival. And they had to tell them that it will be late. So he was swamped with work. And I remember standing with my sister and some other women, talking, and he came and joined us and started talking to us. And then he asked me if-- if I could help him. He asked me if I can work an adding machine and whatnot. And I said, well, I never did, but if he showed me how to, I'm sure I can do it. And I helped him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then before we arrived in New York, he said he would like to see me, and would I call him? Because I didn't know where I would be. And I was met with-- by relatives-- and the-- by my cousin who lived in New York, and he sent us directly to Pittsburgh. So I didn't get a chance to call him. He lived in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: But while-- while I was in Pittsburgh, I had written to him and called him one time. And he only stayed in New York for a week or so between trips to Europe. So I called him, and then he left for his trip. And this was in February.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in May of that same year, we left-- my sister and I left Pittsburgh, came back to New York. So I thought, I'll give him a call again. And I did, and we got together. And I saw him, like, once a month when he came back from his trip. And then in September when he came back, he said he wants to leave the ship and the Merchant Marines and wants to get a job in New York, settle down, and would I marry him? And this wonderful man--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were married in November. That same--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this wonderful man's name? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Bernie Anolik. And we had a wonderful, wonderful life since then-- a wonderful family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and Bernie got married in October?

speaker: interviewee
text: In November.

speaker: interviewer
text: November.

speaker: interviewee
text: November 28, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you settled in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York for-- we were in New York for about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one year. Yeah-- close to it, not even-- not even a whole year. We lived in New York, and then we moved to New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have two wonderful children. I have a daughter, Miriam. She was born in 1949 in New York. And I had a son who was born in New Jersey-- in Hackensack, New Jersey-- in 1952-- September 20, 1952. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have four wonderful grandchildren. My daughter has three boys-- David, Joshua, and Daniel. And my son Jonathan has a girl, Jennifer. She's eight years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you and your husband do after you settled in New Jersey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we lived in New Jersey, but he worked in New York. He worked for RCA Institutes. He taught electronics. He was an electrical engineer. And we lived in-- at first we lived in Duma, New Jersey for two years, until we bought a house in River Edge, New Jersey. Stayed there for four years, and then we moved to another house-- a new house-- in Bergenfield, where we lived for 31 years before moving to Cherry Hill.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you active in any survivors' groups currently?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you a member of a synagogue currently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- member of Temple Beth Sholom, a conservative synagogue. And-- I'm not very active, but fairly active. I'm definitely involved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Erna, you never told us what you did. Did you ever go back to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, after-- when my children were grown. Miriam was in college, and Jonathan was about to go to college, I decided, I'll go out to work. And I-- I worked for Burlington Industries as a textile engineer-- textile planner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. Did you ever meet Mr. Niermann again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1970, Bernie and I went back to Europe for the first time. We had a trip that was organized by RCA for the employees. And we went, took a-- a two-week trip which went to England then Holland and to Paris. No, I think-- yes. England, Holland, and Paris. While we were in Holland, we were so close to Essen that I-- I asked Bernie-- and we were driving-- so I said, why don't we drive into Essen and just visit the Niermanns? I-- so we did. We drove into Essen, and I just called them. They didn't know where-- where I was, that I was in Europe. They didn't know I was coming. And I called Mr. Niermann. And at that time, their older daughter was very sick, and his wife was at-- at the daughter's house. So he called her, asked her to come home because we are here. And he was absolutely delight-- it was like a long-lost daughter came home. He was just so happy to see me. He was just wonderful, delightful. So we spent just a couple of hours with-- with him-- with him and his wife. And it was just wonderful, wonderful. Several years later, I went back to Europe again. In the meantime, he died. But we went back, and I visited his wife, his daughter-- the one who was sick in 1970 died also. And-- but he had-- his younger daughter survived, and his wife lived with the daughter. So we visit-- had a lovely, lovely visit with them one time also. It was a wonderful family-- wonderful family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to your old hometown in Uzhorod?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not. I did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to Essen later on in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1981, I went back. In 1981, I had a letter from the former synagogue of Essen, asking me-- telling me that they are converting the synagogue, into-- making it into a Jewish museum. And would I come for the opening of the Jewish museum, having been in camp in Essen? So I said I would. And my husband was working, had a job in Israel at that time. And I was with him in Israel, so I went from Israel into Germany. I got on the plane in Tel Aviv, a Lufthansa. And there were large group of people, and I heard somebody speaking Hungarian. And I went over to talk to them to find out who they were and where they're going. I found they were two women who were in camp with me at-- working at Krupp. One of them married a man from-- a Jewish man from Essen, and they were-- and she lived on a kibbutz in Israel. The other woman-- so she was going home, with-- back to Essen, with her husband. Because he was a-- an Essen-- former Essen resident, and they invited to the museum opening, residents-- residents-- former residents of Essen-- Jewish residents. They invited about a dozen people from Israel. This woman went with her husband. There was another woman. She apparently lived in Sweden all the time, but she was now in-- in Israel. Her husband died, and she went back to Israel. And she was very sick, but she was also going to the opening of the museum. And when I told them who I was, they could not believe. Because apparently, they were told that-- after we have escape-- we had escaped from camp, the SS people told them that they caught-- that we were caught and we were hung. So they thought that we were dead. So they were just-- they couldn't believe that I was one of the six people who escaped, that I was actually alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, do you keep in contact with any of the others? I know your sister--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --but the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other two, yes. They both live in-- in Florida, and I speak to Rene occasionally. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Also, I-- I have to mention the-- when we-- when I went back to Germany for the opening of the museum, I also visited with Mrs. Niermann and their daughter at that time also. And that was a very interesting experience, because they had schoolchildren, classes of children, come to the-- to the museum, to the synagogue, and had us talk to them. And they were very interested to see-- to hear what happened. And they all said that their parents or grandparents never talked about it. They never knew about it-- never knew what happened. And I told them that that couldn't possibly be because here we were marching-- every day, every morning-- from our camp to the factory in the green-- I mean the gray-- dress that we got still in Auschwitz, looking the way we did. People had to see us. They had to see us coming back and forth from the factory to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lucky coincidences-- that's all. That's all I can attribute it to. Because first of all, I was lucky that I was at the age I was at. I was in my prime of my life. Being 20 years old, I was very healthy, so I was picked out to go to work rather than put into-- into the gas chamber. So that was one lucky coincidence. I was lucky that when we were in camp in Essen, the camp was bombed, but it was bombed during the day when we were working. We came back, and there-- there was a-- another women's camp-- I think it was Polish prisoners of war-- camp next to ours. And that was bombed, and many women were killed. We came back to our camp. We saw legs, and arms, and whatnot from people who were killed. And we were saved. [INAUDIBLE] I mean, it was just a coincidence. Also in-- in-- at work, the factory was bombed. And-- so many times-- even our camp was bombed. And just being at the right place, at the right time-- even when we escaped and we were hiding in the-- in the cemetery. We were in this building. One side of the building was bombed and collapsed, and we were in the other side of the building. So I guess just-- just being lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a message or a lesson for your children or grandchildren from this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just hope they never have to experience anything like that ever, anywhere. Not only they, but any people just because of their religion or whatever. And they have to remember that Jews went through a lot and-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and I'm very glad that I-- that my children are very good Jewish people. They-- they both belong to synagogues, conservative synagogues. They have wonderful Jewish homes. They bring up their children Jewish, and I just hope it stays that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: We appreciate you telling your story. Why did you-- why are you willing to tell us your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have never told my story to my children or grandchildren. I guess in the beginning when my children were growing up, I didn't want to revisit my life-- that life. I didn't want to tell them about-- I never wanted to talk about it. I'm sure they knew because occasionally they would ask about grandparents or relatives or families, so I-- But I never went into details telling them the story. They were probably afraid to ask me questions because they didn't want to hurt me. And the same with the grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- but I think that-- I know that I know very little about my parents and my grandparents, and unfortunately, there's nobody to ask question of. And I think that maybe when-- by the time my-- my grandchildren would be interested in knowing what-- about their ancestry or their grandparents or whatever, there won't be anybody around to ask questions and tell them about it. So I thought, well, maybe if it's recorded and if they're interested, they can find information there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think it's good for Jewish people in general to know what happened, that everybody should remember it-- especially here when there are so many people saying that it-- it never happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I thank you very much. You were wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Erna, could you explain who is in this photo, please, and the significance of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a class photograph of my Hebrew school gymnasium class. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the center, there's the professor. His name was Dr. [? Farkas. ?] And as you see, all the boys are wearing their uniform caps. Since it was a Hebrew gymnasium, they couldn't go bareheaded anyway. But they were-- they had a-- a cap that girls didn't have to wear, but the boys did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in this middle row, third from the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we're going to take the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of-- in the forefront is my grandfather and grandmother, Herman and Rebecca Roth. And behind my grandfather, to the left, is my Aunt Helen from Pittsburgh-- the one who sponsored our trip to the United States. And behind my grandmother are three of my aunts. The one in the middle, between my grandmother and grandfather, is my Aunt Ida who lived on the same street as we did-- my father's sister. And to the right is my father, holding my cousin [? Mary Anne. ?] My Aunt Helen, and [? Mary Anne ?] and her sister [? Sarah ?] [? Anne, ?] and my uncle came-- this picture must have been taken in 1932 because they came to visit, and they brought their daughter [? Mary Anne ?] for an operation in Vienna. And while they were in Vienna, they came to visit the family. This is a photograph taken in 1981 when I came to-- back to Essen for the opening of the museum. All the way to the right is the woman who was in charge of the museum. I'm standing next to her. Next to me is the woman who used to-- both women with me were in camp with me in Essen. I didn't know them. I met them on the plane going from Israel to Germany. The one next to me used to live in Sweden. After the war, she lived in Sweden. Her husband died, and then she went back to Israel. The other lady on the left married a-- a man from Essen, and they live on a kibbutz in Israel. And she was also going back.
